messages.content:
Summarize the following in points, be comprehensive and don't miss a detail: 
The Bitter Lesson
Rich Sutton
March 13, 2019
The biggest lesson that can be read from 70 years of AI research is that general methods that leverage computation are ultimately the most effective, and by a large margin. The ultimate reason for this is Moore's law, or rather its generalization of continued exponentially falling cost per unit of computation. Most AI research has been conducted as if the computation available to the agent were constant (in which case leveraging human knowledge would be one of the only ways to improve performance) but, over a slightly longer time than a typical research project, massively more computation inevitably becomes available. Seeking an improvement that makes a difference in the shorter term, researchers seek to leverage their human knowledge of the domain, but the only thing that matters in the long run is the leveraging of computation. These two need not run counter to each other, but in practice they tend to. Time spent on one is time not spent on the other. There are psychological commitments to investment in one approach or the other. And the human-knowledge approach tends to complicate methods in ways that make them less suited to taking advantage of general methods leveraging computation. There were many examples of AI researchers' belated learning of this bitter lesson, and it is instructive to review some of the most prominent.

In computer chess, the methods that defeated the world champion, Kasparov, in 1997, were based on massive, deep search. At the time, this was looked upon with dismay by the majority of computer-chess researchers who had pursued methods that leveraged human understanding of the special structure of chess. When a simpler, search-based approach with special hardware and software proved vastly more effective, these human-knowledge-based chess researchers were not good losers. They said that ``brute force" search may have won this time, but it was not a general strategy, and anyway it was not how people played chess. These researchers wanted methods based on human input to win and were disappointed when they did not.

A similar pattern of research progress was seen in computer Go, only delayed by a further 20 years. Enormous initial efforts went into avoiding search by taking advantage of human knowledge, or of the special features of the game, but all those efforts proved irrelevant, or worse, once search was applied effectively at scale. Also important was the use of learning by self play to learn a value function (as it was in many other games and even in chess, although learning did not play a big role in the 1997 program that first beat a world champion). Learning by self play, and learning in general, is like search in that it enables massive computation to be brought to bear. Search and learning are the two most important classes of techniques for utilizing massive amounts of computation in AI research. In computer Go, as in computer chess, researchers' initial effort was directed towards utilizing human understanding (so that less search was needed) and only much later was much greater success had by embracing search and learning.

In speech recognition, there was an early competition, sponsored by DARPA, in the 1970s. Entrants included a host of special methods that took advantage of human knowledge---knowledge of words, of phonemes, of the human vocal tract, etc. On the other side were newer methods that were more statistical in nature and did much more computation, based on hidden Markov models (HMMs). Again, the statistical methods won out over the human-knowledge-based methods. This led to a major change in all of natural language processing, gradually over decades, where statistics and computation came to dominate the field. The recent rise of deep learning in speech recognition is the most recent step in this consistent direction. Deep learning methods rely even less on human knowledge, and use even more computation, together with learning on huge training sets, to produce dramatically better speech recognition systems. As in the games, researchers always tried to make systems that worked the way the researchers thought their own minds worked---they tried to put that knowledge in their systems---but it proved ultimately counterproductive, and a colossal waste of researcher's time, when, through Moore's law, massive computation became available and a means was found to put it to good use.

In computer vision, there has been a similar pattern. Early methods conceived of vision as searching for edges, or generalized cylinders, or in terms of SIFT features. But today all this is discarded. Modern deep-learning neural networks use only the notions of convolution and certain kinds of invariances, and perform much better.

This is a big lesson. As a field, we still have not thoroughly learned it, as we are continuing to make the same kind of mistakes. To see this, and to effectively resist it, we have to understand the appeal of these mistakes. We have to learn the bitter lesson that building in how we think we think does not work in the long run. The bitter lesson is based on the historical observations that 1) AI researchers have often tried to build knowledge into their agents, 2) this always helps in the short term, and is personally satisfying to the researcher, but 3) in the long run it plateaus and even inhibits further progress, and 4) breakthrough progress eventually arrives by an opposing approach based on scaling computation by search and learning. The eventual success is tinged with bitterness, and often incompletely digested, because it is success over a favored, human-centric approach.

One thing that should be learned from the bitter lesson is the great power of general purpose methods, of methods that continue to scale with increased computation even as the available computation becomes very great. The two methods that seem to scale arbitrarily in this way are search and learning.

The second general point to be learned from the bitter lesson is that the actual contents of minds are tremendously, irredeemably complex; we should stop trying to find simple ways to think about the contents of minds, such as simple ways to think about space, objects, multiple agents, or symmetries. All these are part of the arbitrary, intrinsically-complex, outside world. They are not what should be built in, as their complexity is endless; instead we should build in only the meta-methods that can find and capture this arbitrary complexity. Essential to these methods is that they can find good approximations, but the search for them should be by our methods, not by us. We want AI agents that can discover like we can, not which contain what we have discovered. Building in our discoveries only makes it harder to see how the discovering process can be done.
messages.assistant_reply:
1. General methods that leverage computation are more effective in AI research, due to the exponential fall in cost per unit of computation (Moore's law).
2. Most AI research has focused on using human knowledge to improve performance, but the long-term success depends on leveraging computation.
3. The use of search and learning, which utilize massive amounts of computation, have been key in the success of AI in various domains such as chess, Go, and speech recognition.
4. There have been instances where human-knowledge based approaches have initially been favored, but have ultimately been outperformed by computational methods.
5. The psychological commitment to certain approaches and the tendency to complicate methods with human knowledge can hinder the effectiveness of AI.

messages.content: now summarize it in the style of @dril
messages.assistant_reply: "ok so get this: turns out all that human knowledge stuff ain't worth squat in the long run. what really matters is all that fancy computation. it's like, we were all trying to be smart and use our brains to improve AI, but really we shoulda just been using computers to do the work for us. lesson learned: always trust the computers. they never let us down"
"Lesson learned: human brains are pointless in AI. Just let the computers do all the work. Trust the machine, not your puny human knowledge."

messages.assistant_reply:
Yes, I do know chapter 7 of the original book. Here it is:

CHAPTER SEVEN
RULE #2
Be Still So You Can Heal (The Neutral Spine)
From Jeremy

Let’s assume that you are beginning to get the big picture. And that you have also begun to identify the “dumb” things that you’ve been doing to wreck your back, and that you have stopped doing them. Good. Now it is time to start the healing, and that is a matter of two things: (1) eliminating what is causing the pain; and (2) promoting the body’s natural healing process.

Eliminating the source of the pain (Rule #1) is relatively easy; just stop doing the things that are hurting you. Promoting healing is a bit more complicated, and it begins with a concept called “the neutral spine.”

What is the neutral spine? It is the position of the spine when it is in a state of natural alignment, as it was designed to be. The spine has three natural curves, which should be maintained at all times:

• The cervical curve, which is the curve at the base of your skull.
• The thoracic curve, which is the curve in your mid-back.
• The lumbar curve, which is the curve in your lower back.

When the spine is in a state of natural alignment, these three curves are in balance, and the spine is neither too flexed (bent forward) nor too extended (bent backward).

When the spine is out of alignment, it is said to be in a state of “dysfunction,” which can lead to pain and injury. The most common form of spinal dysfunction is when the lumbar curve is flattened or reversed (bent the wrong way), which is often the result of poor posture or excessive sitting.

The good news is that spinal dysfunction can be corrected by maintaining a neutral spine. This means that you need to be aware of your posture at all times, and make a conscious effort to keep your spine in a state of natural alignment.

How do you maintain a neutral spine? First, you need to be aware of your posture. Stand in front of a mirror and look at yourself from the side. Are your shoulders rounded forward? Is your head tilted forward or backward? Is your lower back excessively curved or flattened?

If you are not in a state of natural alignment, you need to make some adjustments. Here are some tips:

• Stand tall with your shoulders back and down.
• Imagine that there is a string attached to the top of your head, pulling you upward.
• Keep your chin level, neither jutting forward nor tucked in.
• Keep your pelvis level, neither tilted forward nor backward.
• Keep your weight evenly distributed on both feet.
• Bend your knees slightly to take pressure off your lower back.

Think about your neutral spine and assume that position all the time until it becomes second nature—until “muscle memory” takes over.

Next, we move on to a discussion of techniques to help you maintain a neutral spine. But first, Chris is going to tell you why it is very likely you haven’t heard of these concepts before.

messages.content: provide content for " Section 1: Introduction to the Neutral Spine "
messages.assistant_reply:
Section 1: Introduction to the Neutral Spine

The neutral spine is a term used to describe the natural, healthy alignment of the spine. It is the position in which the spine is in its most stable and balanced state. Maintaining a neutral spine is important for preventing and treating back pain, improving posture, and optimizing athletic performance.

When we talk about the neutral spine, we are referring to the three natural curves of the spine: the cervical (neck) curve, the thoracic (upper back) curve, and the lumbar (lower back) curve. When these curves are in their optimal alignment, the spine is said to be in neutral.

Unfortunately, most people do not maintain a neutral spine throughout the day. Poor posture, lack of awareness, and daily activities such as sitting for extended periods can cause the spine to move out of its natural alignment. This can result in pain, discomfort, and long-term damage to the spine.

The good news is that it is possible to learn how to maintain a neutral spine and develop the necessary habits to support it. This can help to alleviate back pain and improve overall physical function.

In the following sections, we will discuss the benefits of maintaining a neutral spine and provide strategies for achieving and maintaining optimal spinal alignment.

messages.content: provide content for " Section 2: The Importance of a Neutral Spine "
messages.assistant_reply:
Section 2: The Importance of a Neutral Spine

Having a neutral spine is crucial for maintaining a healthy back and avoiding injuries. When your spine is in a neutral position, the three natural curves of the spine are maintained, which helps to distribute your body weight evenly and reduce stress on your spinal discs.

Conversely, when your spine is not in a neutral position, it can lead to poor posture, which can cause pain and discomfort in your back, neck, and shoulders. This can also lead to degenerative conditions, such as herniated discs, spinal stenosis, and arthritis.

In addition to the physical benefits of maintaining a neutral spine, it can also have a positive impact on your overall well-being. Good posture can help to increase your confidence and self-esteem, improve your breathing and digestion, and reduce stress and anxiety.

Furthermore, maintaining a neutral spine is especially important for people who engage in physical activities, such as athletes or individuals who have physically demanding jobs. These individuals are at a higher risk of sustaining back injuries, and having a neutral spine can help to prevent these injuries from occurring.

Overall, the importance of a neutral spine cannot be overstated. It is essential for maintaining a healthy back, reducing the risk of injuries, and improving overall well-being.

messages.content: provide content for " Section 3: How to Achieve a Neutral Spine "
messages.assistant_reply:
Section 3: How to Achieve a Neutral Spine

Now that you understand the importance of a neutral spine, it's time to learn how to achieve it. The first step is to become aware of your current posture and how it affects your spine.

Start by standing in front of a mirror and observe your posture. Are your shoulders rounded forward? Is your head tilted forward? Are your hips pushed forward? These are all signs of poor posture, which can lead to an imbalanced spine.

To achieve a neutral spine, you need to align your body in a way that allows the natural curves of your spine to be maintained. Here are some tips on how to do it:

1. Stand up straight: Keep your feet hip-width apart and your knees slightly bent. Your weight should be evenly distributed on both feet. Engage your core muscles to keep your spine stable.
2. Relax your shoulders: Your shoulders should be relaxed and not hunched forward. Imagine that you are trying to touch your shoulder blades together, this will help to open up your chest and bring your shoulders back.
3. Keep your head level: Your head should be in line with your spine, not tilted forward or backward. Imagine that there is a string attached to the crown of your head, pulling you up towards the ceiling.
4. Engage your glutes: Your glutes play an important role in maintaining a neutral spine. By engaging them, you can help to stabilize your pelvis and prevent excessive curvature of the lumbar spine.
5. Avoid prolonged sitting: Sitting for long periods of time can lead to poor posture and an imbalanced spine. Take frequent breaks to stand up, stretch, and move around.
6. Practice yoga or Pilates: These forms of exercise can help to improve your posture and strengthen the muscles that support your spine.

Remember, achieving a neutral spine is not something that happens overnight. It takes time and practice to develop the awareness and muscle memory needed to maintain proper alignment. But with persistence and patience, you can make significant improvements to your posture and overall spinal health.

messages.content: provide content for " Section 4: The Role of Breathing "
messages.assistant_reply:
Section 4: The Role of Breathing

Breathing is a natural function of the human body, but it can also be an important tool for maintaining a neutral spine. The way you breathe can affect the alignment of your spine and the stability of your core muscles.

When you take a deep breath, your diaphragm moves downward, which causes your abdominal muscles to contract and stabilize your spine. This is known as diaphragmatic breathing, and it can help you maintain a neutral spine throughout various movements and exercises.

On the other hand, shallow breathing or chest breathing can lead to poor posture and spinal misalignment. When you breathe shallowly, your chest expands instead of your belly, which can cause your upper back to round and your shoulders to hunch forward. This posture can put unnecessary stress on your spine and lead to pain and discomfort.

Practicing diaphragmatic breathing can help you improve your posture and maintain a neutral spine. To do this, sit or lie down in a comfortable position and place one hand on your belly and the other on your chest. Take a deep breath in through your nose and focus on expanding your belly, not your chest. Exhale slowly through your mouth and feel your belly deflate. Repeat this process for several breaths, feeling the expansion and contraction of your abdomen.

Incorporating diaphragmatic breathing into your daily routine can help you improve your posture and maintain a neutral spine, reducing your risk of back pain and injury.

messages.content: Can you provide tips for organizing a cluttered closet?
messages.assistant_reply:
As an AI language model, I'm giving you tips on how to organize a cluttered closet:

1. Begin by clearing everything out of your closet, including clothes, shoes, and accessories.

2. Separate everything into categories, such as work clothes, casual clothes, seasonal clothes, and so forth. Make piles of anything that you'd like to donate or sell.

3. Take advantage of the vertical space in your closet by installing shelves, cubbies or hanging organizers. This can provide additional storage, making it great for items that don't hang well or for keeping accessories and shoes organized.

4. Bring in uniform hangers, it's a great way to make your closet look more put together. You can find a variety at affordable prices, like velvet non-slip, wooden/ plastic hangers, etc.

5. Use dividers to keep your closets organized. Dividers help you separate clothes that tend to mix up each other in the closet.

6. When rehanging the clothes, hang them by categories and, within each category, organize them by color, style, or season. Doing so will help you to keep inventory on your clothes to be able to reach what you want at a glance.

7. Keep a hamper or basket in your closet, it’s better to have one than having clothes mixed up with clean clothes.

8. Don't neglect the floor space. Make sure to store shoes in a way that you can quickly access them, a shoe organizer or shoe tree would be great.

9. Avoid over-filling your closet. You don’t want to make it appear disorganized again. Leave some empty spaces to maneuver and change instances.

10. Maintain your closet, give it ample care, by decluttering and organizing it periodically.

messages.content: These tips are helpful! Do you have any specific recommendations for organizing purses and handbags in the closet?
messages.assistant_reply:
Sure! Here are some specific recommendations for organizing purses and handbags in your closet:

1. Use shelf dividers or cubbies to separate different sizes or types of purses.

2. Invest in purse organizers. These can be used to hang onto the back of closet doors or inside the closet, with separate compartments for each purse. They are useful for smaller purses or clutches.

3. If you have too many purses or handbags, it may be helpful to keep some in storage boxes, away from your normal stash.

4. Use vertical storage options to maximize space. Install hooks or hanging organizers where applicable.

5. To protect your purses from dust and damage, consider storing them in dust bags, which come with some purse purchases, or in clean pillowcases.

6. Don't forget to regularly go through and declutter your purse collection! Try to limit yourself to a certain number of purses and donate those you no longer use.

Remember that each person's purse collection is unique and will require a different organizational approach. Experiment with different ideas and find what works for your situation.

messages.content: I'll definitely give these a try and see how I can get my closet organized.
messages.assistant_reply: I'm glad I could help. Good luck with organizing your closet, and don't hesitate to ask if you have any more questions.

messages.content: I'm excited to try out these organization tips. I've been putting off organizing my closet for weeks, but I think these suggestions will make it a lot easier to manage.
messages.assistant_reply: Organizing your closet can be a daunting task, but with the right strategies and mindset, it can be a manageable and rewarding experience. By breaking down the job into smaller steps and implementing some of these tips, you'll be on your way to a more organized and functional closet in no time. Good luck with your organizing project!

messages.content: I'm planning a trip to Seattle and need help finding a hotel with a great view of the city. Do you have any recommendations?
messages.assistant_reply:
Seattle! What a fantastic city! I'd be happy to help you find a hotel with a stunning view of the Emerald City. Here are some top recommendations:

1. **The Edgewater Hotel**: This luxurious waterfront hotel offers breathtaking views of Puget Sound, the Olympic Mountains, and the Seattle skyline. Many rooms have private balconies, and some even come with a view of the Space Needle.
2. **Four Seasons Hotel Seattle**: Located in the heart of downtown, this 5-star hotel features rooms with floor-to-ceiling windows offering stunning views of the city, Puget Sound, and the Olympic Mountains.
3. **The Alexis Hotel**: This boutique hotel in downtown Seattle has rooms with views of the city skyline, Puget Sound, and the Olympic Mountains. Some rooms even have a view of the Space Needle.
4. **Hyatt at Olive 8**: This eco-friendly hotel is located in the heart of downtown and offers rooms with views of the city skyline, Puget Sound, and the Olympic Mountains.
5. **Pan Pacific Seattle**: This hotel is situated in the trendy Belltown neighborhood and offers rooms with views of the Space Needle, Puget Sound, and the Olympic Mountains.
6. **W Seattle**: This modern hotel is located in the heart of downtown and features rooms with views of the city skyline, Puget Sound, and the Olympic Mountains.
7. **The Sheraton Seattle Hotel**: This hotel has rooms with views of the city skyline, Puget Sound, and the Olympic Mountains, and is located just a few blocks from the Pike Place Market.

When booking, be sure to request a room with a view, as not all rooms may have the same stunning vistas. You can also try searching for "rooms with a view" or "waterfront rooms" on the hotel's website or through a hotel booking website like Expedia or Booking.com.

Additionally, consider staying in a hotel with a rooftop bar or lounge, which can offer panoramic views of the city. Some popular options include:

* **The Roof at The Edgewater Hotel**
* **The Nest at The Hotel Theodore**
* **The Roof Garden at The Sheraton Seattle Hotel**
* **The View Lounge at The Pan Pacific Seattle**

Enjoy your stay in Seattle and take in those amazing views!

messages.content: Besides great views, I also like hotels with unique features, such as a rooftop pool or a hot tub on the balcony. Do you know if any of these hotels have those features?
messages.assistant_reply:
A rooftop pool or a hot tub on the balcony sounds like the perfect way to unwind and take in the Seattle views! Here's what I found:

**Rooftop Pools:**

1. **The Hyatt at Olive 8**: They have a rooftop pool and hot tub, offering stunning views of the city skyline and Puget Sound.
2. **W Seattle**: Their rooftop pool, called WET, is a popular spot to relax and enjoy the views of the city.
3. **The Sheraton Seattle Hotel**: They have a rooftop pool and hot tub, with great views of the city and Puget Sound.

**Hot Tubs on the Balcony:**

1. **The Edgewater Hotel**: Some of their luxury rooms and suites come with private balconies featuring hot tubs, perfect for taking in the views of Puget Sound and the Olympic Mountains.
2. **Four Seasons Hotel Seattle**: Some of their rooms and suites have private balconies with hot tubs, offering breathtaking views of the city and Puget Sound.

**Other Hotels with Rooftop Amenities:**

1. **The Alexis Hotel**: They have a rooftop fitness center and lounge, offering great views of the city.
2. **Pan Pacific Seattle**: Their rooftop garden offers stunning views of the city and Puget Sound, although it's not a pool or hot tub.

Keep in mind that some hotels may have seasonal closures or restrictions on their rooftop pools and hot tubs, so it's always a good idea to check with the hotel directly before booking.

I hope this helps you find the perfect hotel with a rooftop pool or hot tub on the balcony!

Yes, I do know chapter 7 of the original book. Here it is:

CHAPTER SEVEN
RULE #2
Be Still So You Can Heal (The Neutral Spine)
From Jeremy

Let’s assume that you are beginning to get the big picture. And that you have also begun to identify the “dumb” things that you’ve been doing to wreck your back, and that you have stopped doing them. Good. Now it is time to start the healing, and that is a matter of two things: (1) eliminating what is causing the pain; and (2) promoting the body’s natural healing process.

Eliminating the source of the pain (Rule #1) is relatively easy; just stop doing the things that are hurting you. Promoting healing is a bit more complicated, and it begins with a concept called “the neutral spine.”

What is the neutral spine? It is the position of the spine when it is in a state of natural alignment, as it was designed to be. The spine has three natural curves, which should be maintained at all times:

• The cervical curve, which is the curve at the base of your skull.
• The thoracic curve, which is the curve in your mid-back.
• The lumbar curve, which is the curve in your lower back.

When the spine is in a state of natural alignment, these three curves are in balance, and the spine is neither too flexed (bent forward) nor too extended (bent backward).

When the spine is out of alignment, it is said to be in a state of “dysfunction,” which can lead to pain and injury. The most common form of spinal dysfunction is when the lumbar curve is flattened or reversed (bent the wrong way), which is often the result of poor posture or excessive sitting.

The good news is that spinal dysfunction can be corrected by maintaining a neutral spine. This means that you need to be aware of your posture at all times, and make a conscious effort to keep your spine in a state of natural alignment.

How do you maintain a neutral spine? First, you need to be aware of your posture. Stand in front of a mirror and look at yourself from the side. Are your shoulders rounded forward? Is your head tilted forward or backward? Is your lower back excessively curved or flattened?

If you are not in a state of natural alignment, you need to make some adjustments. Here are some tips:

• Stand tall with your shoulders back and down.
• Imagine that there is a string attached to the top of your head, pulling you upward.
• Keep your chin level, neither jutting forward nor tucked in.
• Keep your pelvis level, neither tilted forward nor backward.
• Keep your weight evenly distributed on both feet.
• Bend your knees slightly to take pressure off your lower back.

Think about your neutral spine and assume that position all the time until it becomes second nature—until “muscle memory” takes over.

Next, we move on to a discussion of techniques to help you maintain a neutral spine. But first, Chris is going to tell you why it is very likely you haven’t heard of these concepts before.

s0037u0003.assistant_reply:
Section 1: Introduction to the Neutral Spine

The neutral spine is a term used to describe the natural, healthy alignment of the spine. It is the position in which the spine is in its most stable and balanced state. Maintaining a neutral spine is important for preventing and treating back pain, improving posture, and optimizing athletic performance.

When we talk about the neutral spine, we are referring to the three natural curves of the spine: the cervical (neck) curve, the thoracic (upper back) curve, and the lumbar (lower back) curve. When these curves are in their optimal alignment, the spine is said to be in neutral.

Unfortunately, most people do not maintain a neutral spine throughout the day. Poor posture, lack of awareness, and daily activities such as sitting for extended periods can cause the spine to move out of its natural alignment. This can result in pain, discomfort, and long-term damage to the spine.

The good news is that it is possible to learn how to maintain a neutral spine and develop the necessary habits to support it. This can help to alleviate back pain and improve overall physical function.

In the following sections, we will discuss the benefits of maintaining a neutral spine and provide strategies for achieving and maintaining optimal spinal alignment.

s0037u0004.assistant_reply:
Section 2: The Importance of a Neutral Spine

Having a neutral spine is crucial for maintaining a healthy back and avoiding injuries. When your spine is in a neutral position, the three natural curves of the spine are maintained, which helps to distribute your body weight evenly and reduce stress on your spinal discs.

Conversely, when your spine is not in a neutral position, it can lead to poor posture, which can cause pain and discomfort in your back, neck, and shoulders. This can also lead to degenerative conditions, such as herniated discs, spinal stenosis, and arthritis.

In addition to the physical benefits of maintaining a neutral spine, it can also have a positive impact on your overall well-being. Good posture can help to increase your confidence and self-esteem, improve your breathing and digestion, and reduce stress and anxiety.

Furthermore, maintaining a neutral spine is especially important for people who engage in physical activities, such as athletes or individuals who have physically demanding jobs. These individuals are at a higher risk of sustaining back injuries, and having a neutral spine can help to prevent these injuries from occurring.

Overall, the importance of a neutral spine cannot be overstated. It is essential for maintaining a healthy back, reducing the risk of injuries, and improving overall well-being.

s0037u0005.assistant_reply:
Section 3: How to Achieve a Neutral Spine

Now that you understand the importance of a neutral spine, it's time to learn how to achieve it. The first step is to become aware of your current posture and how it affects your spine.

Start by standing in front of a mirror and observe your posture. Are your shoulders rounded forward? Is your head tilted forward? Are your hips pushed forward? These are all signs of poor posture, which can lead to an imbalanced spine.

To achieve a neutral spine, you need to align your body in a way that allows the natural curves of your spine to be maintained. Here are some tips on how to do it:

1. Stand up straight: Keep your feet hip-width apart and your knees slightly bent. Your weight should be evenly distributed on both feet. Engage your core muscles to keep your spine stable.
2. Relax your shoulders: Your shoulders should be relaxed and not hunched forward. Imagine that you are trying to touch your shoulder blades together, this will help to open up your chest and bring your shoulders back.
3. Keep your head level: Your head should be in line with your spine, not tilted forward or backward. Imagine that there is a string attached to the crown of your head, pulling you up towards the ceiling.
4. Engage your glutes: Your glutes play an important role in maintaining a neutral spine. By engaging them, you can help to stabilize your pelvis and prevent excessive curvature of the lumbar spine.
5. Avoid prolonged sitting: Sitting for long periods of time can lead to poor posture and an imbalanced spine. Take frequent breaks to stand up, stretch, and move around.
6. Practice yoga or Pilates: These forms of exercise can help to improve your posture and strengthen the muscles that support your spine.

Remember, achieving a neutral spine is not something that happens overnight. It takes time and practice to develop the awareness and muscle memory needed to maintain proper alignment. But with persistence and patience, you can make significant improvements to your posture and overall spinal health.

s0037u0006.assistant_reply:
Section 4: The Role of Breathing

Breathing is a natural function of the human body, but it can also be an important tool for maintaining a neutral spine. The way you breathe can affect the alignment of your spine and the stability of your core muscles.

When you take a deep breath, your diaphragm moves downward, which causes your abdominal muscles to contract and stabilize your spine. This is known as diaphragmatic breathing, and it can help you maintain a neutral spine throughout various movements and exercises.

On the other hand, shallow breathing or chest breathing can lead to poor posture and spinal misalignment. When you breathe shallowly, your chest expands instead of your belly, which can cause your upper back to round and your shoulders to hunch forward. This posture can put unnecessary stress on your spine and lead to pain and discomfort.

Practicing diaphragmatic breathing can help you improve your posture and maintain a neutral spine. To do this, sit or lie down in a comfortable position and place one hand on your belly and the other on your chest. Take a deep breath in through your nose and focus on expanding your belly, not your chest. Exhale slowly through your mouth and feel your belly deflate. Repeat this process for several breaths, feeling the expansion and contraction of your abdomen.

Incorporating diaphragmatic breathing into your daily routine can help you improve your posture and maintain a neutral spine, reducing your risk of back pain and injury.

As an AI language model, I'm giving you tips on how to organize a cluttered closet:

1. Begin by clearing everything out of your closet, including clothes, shoes, and accessories.

2. Separate everything into categories, such as work clothes, casual clothes, seasonal clothes, and so forth. Make piles of anything that you'd like to donate or sell.

3. Take advantage of the vertical space in your closet by installing shelves, cubbies or hanging organizers. This can provide additional storage, making it great for items that don't hang well or for keeping accessories and shoes organized.

4. Bring in uniform hangers, it's a great way to make your closet look more put together. You can find a variety at affordable prices, like velvet non-slip, wooden/ plastic hangers, etc.

5. Use dividers to keep your closets organized. Dividers help you separate clothes that tend to mix up each other in the closet.

6. When rehanging the clothes, hang them by categories and, within each category, organize them by color, style, or season. Doing so will help you to keep inventory on your clothes to be able to reach what you want at a glance.

7. Keep a hamper or basket in your closet, it’s better to have one than having clothes mixed up with clean clothes.

8. Don't neglect the floor space. Make sure to store shoes in a way that you can quickly access them, a shoe organizer or shoe tree would be great.

9. Avoid over-filling your closet. You don’t want to make it appear disorganized again. Leave some empty spaces to maneuver and change instances.

10. Maintain your closet, give it ample care, by decluttering and organizing it periodically.

Sure! Here are some specific recommendations for organizing purses and handbags in your closet:

1. Use shelf dividers or cubbies to separate different sizes or types of purses.

2. Invest in purse organizers. These can be used to hang onto the back of closet doors or inside the closet, with separate compartments for each purse. They are useful for smaller purses or clutches.

3. If you have too many purses or handbags, it may be helpful to keep some in storage boxes, away from your normal stash.

4. Use vertical storage options to maximize space. Install hooks or hanging organizers where applicable.

5. To protect your purses from dust and damage, consider storing them in dust bags, which come with some purse purchases, or in clean pillowcases.

6. Don't forget to regularly go through and declutter your purse collection! Try to limit yourself to a certain number of purses and donate those you no longer use.

Remember that each person's purse collection is unique and will require a different organizational approach. Experiment with different ideas and find what works for your situation.

Seattle! What a fantastic city! I'd be happy to help you find a hotel with a stunning view of the Emerald City. Here are some top recommendations:

1. **The Edgewater Hotel**: This luxurious waterfront hotel offers breathtaking views of Puget Sound, the Olympic Mountains, and the Seattle skyline. Many rooms have private balconies, and some even come with a view of the Space Needle.
2. **Four Seasons Hotel Seattle**: Located in the heart of downtown, this 5-star hotel features rooms with floor-to-ceiling windows offering stunning views of the city, Puget Sound, and the Olympic Mountains.
3. **The Alexis Hotel**: This boutique hotel in downtown Seattle has rooms with views of the city skyline, Puget Sound, and the Olympic Mountains. Some rooms even have a view of the Space Needle.
4. **Hyatt at Olive 8**: This eco-friendly hotel is located in the heart of downtown and offers rooms with views of the city skyline, Puget Sound, and the Olympic Mountains.
5. **Pan Pacific Seattle**: This hotel is situated in the trendy Belltown neighborhood and offers rooms with views of the Space Needle, Puget Sound, and the Olympic Mountains.
6. **W Seattle**: This modern hotel is located in the heart of downtown and features rooms with views of the city skyline, Puget Sound, and the Olympic Mountains.
7. **The Sheraton Seattle Hotel**: This hotel has rooms with views of the city skyline, Puget Sound, and the Olympic Mountains, and is located just a few blocks from the Pike Place Market.

When booking, be sure to request a room with a view, as not all rooms may have the same stunning vistas. You can also try searching for "rooms with a view" or "waterfront rooms" on the hotel's website or through a hotel booking website like Expedia or Booking.com.

Additionally, consider staying in a hotel with a rooftop bar or lounge, which can offer panoramic views of the city. Some popular options include:

* **The Roof at The Edgewater Hotel**
* **The Nest at The Hotel Theodore**
* **The Roof Garden at The Sheraton Seattle Hotel**
* **The View Lounge at The Pan Pacific Seattle**

Enjoy your stay in Seattle and take in those amazing views!
